title: Architecture Overview
content: Architecture Overview System diagram Packages Message pipeline Event system Database @ownpilot/core @ownpilot/gateway @ownpilot/ui @ownpilot/cli OwnPilot is a TypeScript monorepo built with Turborepo. Four packages work together to deliver a complete self-hosted AI assistant platform. React 19 + Vite 7 + Tailwind CSS 4 frontend. Code-split with lazy loading, dark mode, 120+ components, real-time WebSocket updates. ~40K LOC with 57+ pages. Commander.js CLI for server management, bot control, workspace operations, configuration, and channel management. All messages — whether from the web UI, Telegram, WhatsApp, or triggers — flow through the same MessageBus middleware pipeline: Single-port production In production, port 8080 serves everything: the bundled React UI (static assets), the REST API (/api/v1/*), WebSocket (/ws), and Server-Sent Events. No separate nginx or reverse proxy required for basic deployments.

title: Configuration
content: Configuration Configuration priority Config Center (web UI) CLI configuration REST API All environment variables Agent runtime defaults Model routing Workspace configuration Config API endpoints Core server variables Database variables Authentication variables Configure different AI providers and models for different processes. This lets you use a fast cheap model for workflows while using a powerful model for chat. No restart needed Most configuration changes via the Config Center take effect immediately without restarting the server. AI provider keys, model routing, and agent defaults all hot-reload. Production authentication For any internet-facing deployment, set AUTH_TYPE=jwt and use a strong JWT_SECRET (64+ random characters). Without authentication, anyone who can reach port 8080 has full access to your assistant and personal data. 8080 No HTTP server port 0.0.0.0 No Bind address development No info No error | warn | info | debug localhost Yes PostgreSQL hostname 25432 No Port (Docker maps container 5432 → host 25432) ownpilot Yes Database user — Yes Database password ownpilot No Database name none No none | api-key | jwt — If api-key Static API key for Bearer auth — If jwt JWT signing secret (min 64 characters) 7d No JWT token expiry (e.g., 1h, 7d, 30d) List all keys (values masked) Get specific value Set a value Set multiple values Delete a key List configured services Configure a service (Telegram, etc.) Set model routing config

title: Introduction
content: Introduction What is OwnPilot? Core principles Key numbers Architecture overview What you can do with OwnPilot Quickstart Next steps {title} OwnPilot is a privacy-first personal AI assistant platform that runs entirely on your own infrastructure. Self-hosted. Your data stays yours. OwnPilot is a full-featured AI assistant platform that you deploy on your own hardware or cloud VM. It brings together multi-provider AI (104 providers including local inference), 250+ built-in tools, autonomous soul agents, visual workflow automation, MCP integration, voice, browser automation, IoT control, and Telegram + WhatsApp connectivity — all in a single, self-hosted package. Unlike cloud-based AI assistants, OwnPilot never sends your personal data to third parties. Your conversations, memories, notes, tasks, contacts, and all other personal data stay on your server. OwnPilot is a TypeScript monorepo managed with Turborepo and pnpm. It consists of 4 packages: Quick Start Want to get running immediately? Jump to the{' '} Quick Start guide — Docker gets you live in under 2 minutes. AI Providers 104 (including 8 aggregators + local) Built-in Tools 250+ across 32 categories Workflow Node Types 23 Test Suite 26,500+ tests, 549+ files Codebase ~180K LOC (TypeScript) License MIT Runtime Node.js 22+ Database PostgreSQL 16+ with pgvector

title: Quick Start
description: In dev mode, two URLs are available:
content: Quick Start Option 1: Docker Compose (Recommended) Option 2: Docker with pre-built image Option 3: From source Option 4: Interactive setup wizard Configure AI providers What's available after setup Web UI (recommended) CLI The fastest way to start OwnPilot is with Docker — it pulls the image, starts PostgreSQL with pgvector, and serves everything on a single port. In dev mode, two URLs are available: The wizard guides you through: After starting, configure your AI provider API keys. There are two ways: Prerequisites Docker Desktop (or Docker Engine) must be installed and running. No Node.js required for the Docker path. Custom configuration To customize settings before starting (auth, Telegram token, etc.), copy and edit{' '} .env first: Prerequisites Node.js 22+, pnpm 10+, and Docker (for PostgreSQL) are required. You're ready! Once OwnPilot is running and you've set an API key, navigate to the Chat page to start your first conversation. The assistant has access to all 250+ built-in tools by default. Gateway API REST API + WebSocket + bundled UI UI (dev mode) Vite dev server with hot reload PostgreSQL Database with pgvector extension WebSocket Real-time event broadcasts

title: Agent System Overview
description: On each heartbeat cycle, a soul agent:
content: Agent System Overview Agent types Soul Agents Claw Agents Autonomy Levels Soul agent configuration Heartbeat lifecycle Crew system Scheduling modes Features OwnPilot's agent system has multiple layers — from simple chat agents to fully autonomous soul agents with rich identity and heartbeat lifecycles. Soul agents are autonomous agents with a rich identity framework. They have personality, role, mission, voice, boundaries, and an emoji. They run on a configurable heartbeat schedule and can evolve over time. On each heartbeat cycle, a soul agent: Multiple soul agents can form a crew. Crews enable: Budget enforcement All agent types support budget limits. At 80% of the budget, a warning is logged. At 100%, the agent auto-pauses. Configure per-cycle, per-day, and per-month limits. Chat Agent Conversational agent for web UI, Telegram, WhatsApp On-demand Soul Agent Autonomous agent with identity, heartbeat lifecycle Cron schedule Claw Agent Unified autonomous runtime with .claw/ directives Continuous / interval / event / single-shot Manual All tool executions require explicit approval Assisted Low-risk tools auto-execute; high-risk require approval Supervised Most tools auto-execute; critical patterns blocked Autonomous Full tool access with audit logging Full (Claw) Maximum autonomy for crew orchestration

title: Channels
description: Or via the Config Center API:
content: Channels Supported channels Telegram WhatsApp User approval flow Pairing keys Message formatting Inbox (channel messages) Middleware pipeline Step 1: Create a Telegram bot Step 2: Configure the token Step 3: Start the bot Telegram features Connecting via QR code WhatsApp safety filters Approval settings OwnPilot connects to Telegram and WhatsApp via channel plugins, bringing the full assistant experience — 250+ tools, memory, workflows — directly into your messaging apps. Each channel has approval flow, pairing keys, user management, and inbox persistence. Or via the Config Center API: WhatsApp integration uses the Baileys library for multi-device support. No phone number portability or WhatsApp Business API required — connects your personal WhatsApp. Both Telegram and WhatsApp channels support a user approval system. When a new user messages the bot, they are added to a pending queue. An admin must approve them before they can interact with the assistant. Pairing keys are rotating short codes used to verify channel ownership and link a Telegram/WhatsApp user account to an OwnPilot user account. Channel messages flow through the same middleware pipeline as web UI messages: Channel plugin architecture Channels are implemented as plugins using a builder pattern:{' '} createChannelPlugin().meta().platform().channelApi(factory).build(). The Universal Channel Protocol (UCP) provides a unified message format across channels. Telegram bot in Docker When running in Docker, the Telegram bot starts automatically if{' '} TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN is set. You don't need to run{' '} ownpilot bot start manually. WhatsApp ToS Using unofficial WhatsApp integrations may violate WhatsApp's Terms of Service. Use responsibly and only for personal automation. Shared conversation history A conversation started on Telegram can continue in the web UI, and vice versa. All channels share the same conversation history for a given user. Telegram Production Bot token Full feature support WhatsApp Production QR code scan Multi-device (Baileys) Streaming responses Messages delivered in chunks as the AI generates them Markdown formatting File/image support Photos, documents, and voice messages accepted Inline keyboards Approval dialogs and action buttons sent as inline keyboards Group chats Bot responds when mentioned or in private conversations User approval Only approved users can interact with the bot Anyone can use the bot (no approval required) Only pre-approved users can interact Users request access; admin approves via web UI

title: Coding Agents
content: Coding Agents Supported providers Creating a coding agent Starting a session Streaming output PTY mode Provider configuration Custom provider registration Session management Environment sanitization Fleet integration Cost tracking Configuration options Execution modes OwnPilot orchestrates Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI as coding agents — autonomous code editors that can read files, run tests, make commits, and complete multi-step programming tasks. Sessions stream output in real-time and support PTY mode for interactive tools. PTY (pseudoterminal) mode allocates a real terminal for the coding agent process. This is required for tools that detect whether they're running in a terminal (like interactive test runners, color output, progress bars). Any CLI tool that accepts a prompt on stdin or as a flag and produces output on stdout can be registered as a custom coding agent provider. Coding agent child processes run with a sanitized copy of the parent environment. This prevents conflicts when OwnPilot itself runs inside an agent environment. Coding agents integrate with Fleet Command for parallel code tasks with dependency tracking. Multiple coding sessions can run concurrently, with dependencies cascading on failure. When to use PTY mode Use PTY mode when running commands that require terminal detection: test runners with watch mode, tools using ANSI colors, interactive CLIs. For simple file operations and non-interactive tasks, PTY mode is not needed. CLAUDECODE env var Claude Code sets CLAUDECODE=1 in its environment. If OwnPilot is itself running inside Claude Code, child coding agent processes will see this variable and refuse to start. OwnPilot automatically removes it (and similar variables) from the sanitized environment passed to child coding agents. Claude Code Recommended — best code quality Codex CLI OpenAI's coding CLI Gemini CLI Google's coding agent Custom user-defined — Any CLI tool with stdin/stdout protocol string claude-code | codex | gemini-cli | custom ID string Working directory for the agent string Model to use for inference number Max tool-use iterations per session (default: 20) string Additional instructions prepended to system string[] Restrict which tools the agent can use number Maximum USD cost per session Agent runs autonomously, making all decisions Agent pauses and asks for confirmation on significant actions

title: Edge Devices & IoT
description: Agents have 6 built-in tools for interacting with IoT devices in natural language:
content: Edge Devices & IoT Architecture MQTT broker setup Registering devices MQTT topic structure Reading telemetry Sending commands Command queue 6 LLM tools for edge devices ESP32 example sketch Full REST API reference WebSocket events Option A: Docker (recommended) Option B: Existing broker Device types Compatible hardware OwnPilot integrates with IoT devices via MQTT (Mosquitto), providing a device registry, telemetry storage, command queue, and 6 LLM tools so your AI assistant can read sensors and control actuators directly in conversation. OwnPilot connects to a Mosquitto MQTT broker. Devices publish telemetry to their topic prefix and subscribe to their command topic. OwnPilot subscribes to all registered device topics on startup and stores telemetry in PostgreSQL. Commands sent to offline devices are queued and delivered when the device reconnects, provided the QoS level is 1 or 2. QoS 0 commands are fire-and-forget. Agents have 6 built-in tools for interacting with IoT devices in natural language: Edge device events are broadcast over the WebSocket connection: Broker configuration Configure the MQTT broker URL in Settings → Config Center → Edge or via the{' '} MQTT_BROKER_URL environment variable. The gateway connects automatically when the URL is set. Natural language device control Once devices are registered, you can control them conversationally: "What's the temperature in the living room?" "Turn on the bedroom fan" "Show me humidity trends for the past week" "Alert me when motion is detected in the garage" ESP32 / ESP8266 Temperature, humidity, motion sensors WiFi → MQTT Raspberry Pi GPIO, camera, complex automation Ethernet/WiFi → MQTT Arduino + ESP Simple sensors and actuators Serial → ESP → MQTT Shelly devices Smart plugs, switches, dimmers Built-in MQTT Zigbee2MQTT Zigbee device bridge USB → MQTT Tasmota devices Flashed smart plugs and relays Built-in MQTT

title: MCP Integration
content: MCP Integration OwnPilot as MCP client OwnPilot as MCP server Configuring MCP servers Listing and inspecting connected servers MCP server management API Transport types Using MCP tools in conversations Popular server presets Connecting Claude Desktop Available tools in Claude Desktop Authentication for the MCP server Connecting Claude Code (CLI) OwnPilot is both an MCP client and an MCP server. As a client, it connects to external MCP servers and uses their tools natively. As a server, it exposes all 250+ built-in tools to Claude Desktop, Claude Code, and any other MCP client. Connect any MCP-compatible tool server to OwnPilot. The tools become immediately available to the agent in all conversations, workflows, and autonomous agents — no restart required. Or via the REST API: Tool discovery MCP tools are discovered automatically when a server connects. They appear in the agent's tool search under their server name. The agent doesn't need any special instructions — it can find and use MCP tools the same way as built-in tools. Tool name encoding MCP requires tool names to contain only alphanumeric characters and underscores. OwnPilot's dot-prefixed namespaces (core.create_note) are automatically converted to double-underscore format (core__create_note) for MCP compatibility. The reverse mapping is applied when the tool is executed. Claude Code transport Claude Code uses "type": "http" (not "streamable-http") for Streamable HTTP transport. Use --mcp-config explicitly — Claude Code does not auto-read .mcp.json in -p (print) mode. List all MCP servers Add MCP server Get server details + tools Remove server Connect/reconnect Disconnect Launch a local process, communicate over stdin/stdout Local tools (filesystem, git, etc.) Streamable HTTP transport Remote MCP servers, services Server-Sent Events transport (legacy) Older MCP servers

title: Personal Data
content: Personal Data Data types overview Notes Tasks Goals Calendar Contacts Memory System Bookmarks Expenses Habits Pomodoro Timer Custom Data All LLM tool names Notes API endpoints Task status values Expense categories OwnPilot provides a complete personal data management system — notes, tasks, goals, calendar, contacts, memories, bookmarks, expenses, habits, and custom data — all stored locally in your PostgreSQL database and accessible via REST API and 50+ LLM tools. Notes support rich text content, tags, pinning, and semantic full-text search powered by PostgreSQL full-text search and pgvector embeddings. Tasks support priorities (low/medium/high/urgent), due dates, recurrence rules, status tracking, and sub-task relationships. Goals support milestone tracking, categories, target dates, and progress updates. The agent can automatically track progress and send reminders. Contacts store people with name, email, phone, company, social links, notes, and custom tags. Full-text search across all fields. The memory system uses pgvector for semantic similarity search. When the agent encounters new information it should remember, it stores it with an embedding. Future queries semantically recall relevant memories. Save URLs with AI-generated summaries, tags, and folder organization. Full-text and semantic search across titles, descriptions, and content. Track personal and business expenses with categories, currencies, and tags. The agent can generate expense summaries, category breakdowns, and monthly reports. Track daily, weekly, or custom-schedule habits. The system calculates streaks, completion rates, and best periods. The agent can send habit reminders and celebrate milestones. Built-in Pomodoro technique support with 25-minute focus sessions, 5-minute breaks, and long break intervals. Session data is stored and statistics are available to the agent. The custom data store allows you (or the agent) to persist arbitrary JSON data under named keys. Useful for storing structured personal data that doesn't fit other categories. Privacy guarantee All personal data lives exclusively in your self-hosted PostgreSQL instance. No personal data is ever sent to third-party services. Sensitive fields use AES-256-GCM encryption. Vector similarity search Memories are retrieved using cosine similarity on 1536-dimension embeddings. The agent automatically injects the top-K most relevant memories into its context at the start of each conversation. Tool access via meta-proxy In conversations, the agent discovers and calls these tools through the meta-tool proxy ( search_tools → get_tool_help → use_tool). You can also call any tool directly via POST /api/v1/tools/execute. Notes Rich text notes with tags and pinning Tasks Tasks with priority, due dates, recurrence Goals Long-term goals with milestones Calendar Events with recurrence rules Contacts People with email, phone, company Memories AI memories with vector similarity search Bookmarks URLs with metadata and folders Ex

title: AI Provider Overview
description: Access hundreds of models through aggregator APIs:
content: AI Provider Overview Provider categories Smart Provider Routing Anthropic Prompt Caching Extended Thinking Model Routing Configuring providers Native providers (4) Aggregator providers (8) Local providers (4+) OpenAI-compatible endpoints Web UI (recommended) CLI OwnPilot supports 104 AI providers through a unified provider abstraction. All providers use the same agent interface — you can switch models without changing any code. Direct API integrations with full feature support including streaming, tool calling, and prompt caching: Access hundreds of models through aggregator APIs: For fully local inference — no internet required for AI: Any provider that exposes an OpenAI-compatible API can be used. This includes xAI (Grok), DeepSeek, Mistral, and hundreds of others. OwnPilot can automatically route requests to the optimal provider based on your strategy: Anthropic's extended thinking is supported for deeper reasoning in complex tasks. Configure the thinking budget tokens per agent: Different processes can use different models with fallback chains: Two methods are available: Local AI for privacy For maximum privacy, configure Ollama with a capable local model (llama3.3, qwen2.5, etc.). All AI processing stays on your machine — no data ever leaves your server. OpenAI GPT-4o, o1, o3, etc. Anthropic Claude 3.x + prompt caching Google Gemini Pro, Flash, etc. Zhipu GLM-4 series cheapest Minimize cost per token fastest Minimize latency smartest Maximize capability (benchmark scores) balanced Optimize cost/quality ratio fallback Try providers in order; use next on failure chat Web UI and channel conversations channel Telegram/WhatsApp messages pulse Proactive autonomy engine

title: Tool System Overview
description: All tools use dot-prefixed namespaces for clear origin tracking:
content: Tool System Overview Meta-tool Proxy Tool namespaces Built-in tools (250+) MCP Integration Extensions Skills Platform Custom Tools Connected Apps (Composio) MCP Client MCP Server 6 Default Extensions OwnPilot provides 250+ built-in tools across 32 categories, plus extensible hooks for custom tools, MCP tools, skills, and extensions. The meta-tool proxy keeps LLM context lean. Instead of sending all 250+ tool definitions to the LLM (which would consume thousands of tokens), OwnPilot uses a meta-tool proxy pattern. Only 4 tools are ever sent to the LLM: All tools use dot-prefixed namespaces for clear origin tracking: Organized across 32 categories: OwnPilot is both an MCP client and server: Connect to external MCP servers and use their tools natively in conversations. Popular servers include Filesystem, GitHub, Brave Search, and more. Skills use the open standard SKILL.md format from AgentSkills.io. They are instruction-based AI knowledge packages that extend the assistant's capabilities. Create new tools at runtime via LLM. Tools are sandboxed JavaScript that can call any of the 250+ built-in tools. Stored in the database and available immediately. 1000+ OAuth app integrations via Composio. Google Drive, GitHub, Slack, Notion, Stripe, Linear, Jira, and hundreds more — all accessible through AI tool calling after OAuth authentication. Why meta-tools? Sending 250+ tool definitions to the LLM would use 15,000+ tokens per request. With meta-tools, the LLM uses ~200 tokens to get the schema for exactly the tool it needs. This reduces cost by ~98% and improves response quality. Built-in tools User-created tools Plugin tools Skill tools MCP server tools Extension tools

title: Workflows
content: Workflows 24 Node Types Workflow example Webhook triggers Workflow Copilot 5 Workflow Templates Safety limits Approval recovery OwnPilot's workflow system provides a visual drag-and-drop builder with 24 node types, a Workflow Copilot for AI-assisted creation, and webhook triggers with HMAC signature validation. LLM Node: JSON mode Set responseFormat: "json" on an LLM node to automatically parse the model's output as JSON. This is useful for classification, extraction, and data transformation tasks. Tool Node Execute any built-in or custom tool Trigger Node Entry point (webhook, schedule, event) LLM Node AI inference with optional JSON output Condition Node If/else branching Code Node Run sandboxed JavaScript/Python Transformer Node Transform data with expressions ForEach Node Iterate over array items HTTP Request Node Call external APIs Delay Node Wait (up to 1 hour safety cap) Switch Node Multi-branch routing Error Handler Node Catch and handle errors Sub-Workflow Node Nest another workflow Approval Node Pause for human approval Sticky Note Documentation node Notification Node Send via channels Parallel Node Fan-out concurrent execution Merge Node Wait for branches (all or first) DataStore Node Key-value persistence across runs Schema Validator JSON schema validation Filter Node Filter arrays by predicate Map Node Transform array items Aggregate Node sum/count/avg/min/max/groupBy Webhook Response Send HTTP response for webhook triggers Claw Agent Trigger autonomous claw agent from workflow

title: Deployment
description: Caddy handles TLS automatically via Let's Encrypt.
content: Deployment Docker (Production) Environment Variables Reverse Proxy (nginx + TLS) Reverse Proxy (Caddy) Build from source (production) Database backup Updating Hardware requirements For internet-facing deployments, place OwnPilot behind nginx with Let's Encrypt TLS. WebSocket proxying and SSE (unbuffered) are both required. Caddy handles TLS automatically via Let's Encrypt. Database migrations All migrations are idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS). Updates apply migrations automatically on startup. Always backup your database before updating. No 8080 HTTP server port No 0.0.0.0 Bind address No development Set to production Yes localhost DB hostname No 25432 DB port Yes ownpilot DB username Yes — DB password (use secret) No ownpilot DB name No none none | api-key | jwt If jwt — JWT signing secret (64+ chars) If api-key — Static API key No info error|warn|info|debug No — For IoT/Edge features Minimal 1 GB 1 core 10 GB Light personal use Recommended 4 GB 2 cores 50 GB Multiple agents, workflows Power user 8 GB+ 4+ cores 100 GB+ Heavy automation, local LLMs

title: Security Overview
description: When an agent executes code, all four layers are applied:
content: Security Overview 4-Layer Code Execution Security Encryption PII Detection & Redaction Tamper-Evident Audit Logs SSRF Protection Authentication Modes Rate Limiting Autonomy Levels Extension Security Audit Channel Security OwnPilot is built with a security-first mindset. Multiple layers of protection ensure that your data stays private and that autonomous agents can't do unintended damage. When an agent executes code, all four layers are applied: All encryption is implemented using only Node.js built-ins — zero external dependencies: OwnPilot detects and can redact 15+ categories of personally identifiable information: All tool executions and significant events are logged in a tamper-evident audit log. Each log entry includes a cryptographic hash that chains to the previous entry, making any modification detectable. OwnPilot prevents Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) attacks through two mechanisms: Sliding window rate limiter with burst support. Configured per-route and globally. Rate limiter state is stored in memory with TTL cleanup to prevent memory leaks. 5 autonomy levels control how much agents can do without human approval: Production security For production deployments, always set AUTH_TYPE=jwt and use a strong JWT_SECRET. If exposing OwnPilot to the internet, place it behind a reverse proxy (nginx/Caddy) with TLS. No authentication required Local development, trusted network Static API key in Authorization header Single-user, scripted access JWT tokens with configurable expiry Multi-user, production deployments Manual All actions require explicit approval Assisted Low-risk auto-execute; high-risk require approval Supervised Most auto-execute; critical patterns blocked Autonomous Full access with audit logging Full (Claw) Maximum autonomy for crew orchestration
